But maybe the core problem isn’t just enforcement — it’s design.

We’re using laws meant for individual misconduct, like the Cybercrime Prevention Act of 2012 and the Safe Spaces Act, to fight coordinated influence networks operating at scale.

If the harm is systemic, the law has to evolve intentionally — not stretch five different statutes to cover something they were never built to handle.

Otherwise, we stay reactive while the machinery stays intact.

At sasabihin nila "joke lang iyon?"
Yung mga nanay na naghahanap ng katotohanan sa mga pulihanang barado ang pintuan — joke iyon? Yung mga bata na lumaking alam na lang na "nanlaban" ang tatay nila kahit wala silang armas — exaggeration iyon? Yung mga pangalan sa listahan — misunderstanding iyon?
Hindi.
At yung pinaka-nakakagalit? The defense never — hindi kahit isang beses — nagbigay ng kapani-paniwalang sagot sa pinaka-simpleng tanong: Kung hindi ito policy, kung hindi ito intentional, kung joke lang at drama lang lahat ito — THEN WHY DID THE MACHINE EXIST? Sino ang naglagay ng mga pangalan sa listahan? Sino ang nagbayad ng cash? Sino ang nag-promote ng mga pulis na pumatay?
Walang sagot. Walang counter-story. Puro "doubt the witness, question the numbers" — pero hindi nila kayang sirain ang pundasyon ng kwento, kasi ang kwento ay totoo.
Yung victims' representatives — doon ako pinaka-nasagad ng emosyon. Kasi habang ang defense ay nagta-talk ng legal technicalities, may mga pamilya doon na paulit-ulit na pinapatay — una sa kalye, pangalawa sa loob ng courtroom — tuwing tinatawag na "minimal" o "self-defense" ang pagkamatay ng kanilang mahal sa buhay.
Pinapatay ng bala. Tinatanggal ng salita. Dalawang beses na namatay.
Ang 60 araw na darating ay hindi lang legal na proseso — ito ay sandali ng katotohanan para sa libu-libong pamilyang naghintay ng matagal na panahon. At kung ang scoreboard ay magsasalita ngayon —
Prosecution: 9/10. May sistema. May ebidensya. May mukha ang krimen.
Defense: 5/10. May lakas ng loob, wala namang lakas ng argumento.
Victims: 9/10. At sila ang pinaka-importanteng boses sa buong hearing na ito — hindi man sila ang magdedesisyon, pero sila ang dahilan kung bakit kailangang magdesisyon.
Ang hustisya ay matagal nang hinihingi. At ngayon, sa unang pagkakataon, mukhang nakikinig ang mundo.

I was at EDSA as a young woman. As we celebrated the success of People Power, I felt an overwhelming joy and naively told myself that my beloved country would be peaceful and prosperous from then on. I thought that the Filipino people have survived the worst time of their lives and that the future would be nothing but bright. How wrong I was. Little did I know that the forces of darkness would come back with a vengeance - industrial scale corruption in government; a serious threat to our sovereignty; traitorous, stupid, and power-hungry politicians; unbelievably deluded, misguided, and ruthless fanatics; and the Devil himself. Quite a bleak scenario...but all is not lost. Call me Pollyanna as I envision a new dawn for the Motherland. We've done it before and we can do it again. For as long as walang bibitaw among us, we will come out of this hell-of-a-predicament triumphant. Kaya laban lang tayo para sa Inang Bayan!!!

I was there in Edsa 40yrs ago with my college classmates, ang ganda ng atmosphere doon nung araw ng feb. 25, 1986 ang saya ng mga tao, nagkaisa dhil gusto ng pagbabago. Ngayon nagbago dhil s kasakiman, puro pansariling interes ang inuuna khit ang kalabasan ng yun ay mawala ang pagmamahal s bansa ntin, ang Bansang Pilipinas 😢

I was really young back then but I remember my dad joining the takeover of malacañang. He even brought home a few books from inside the palace, and I think they’re still on our shelves. I also remember hearing the tora-tora planes flying over our home in Marikina.
Honestly, I feel like the spirit of EDSA revolution is slowly being forgotten. These days, a lot of people seem to care more about what benefits them personally rather than doing what’s right for our country. Sad to say, but it’s the truth.

EDSA 1 was one of the most memorable defining moment of a Pilipino. For the 1st time for the last 2 decades, Pilipinos worldwide were proud to proclaim themselves as I am a Pilipino. Not a Bicolano, Kapampangan, Tagalog, Cebuano nor a Davaoeno.

How proud I was, when a nun bravely offered a flower to a confused and a bewildered soldier manning a tank. Everybody was holding their breath on what will happen next.
Will she be ran over? Will the soldiers fire their guns?

But, Lo and behold. He accepted the flower. People suddenly approached the soldiers as conquering heroes. Bottled water and sandwiches were offered and accepted . For the 1st time since martial law, the soldiers were welcomed.

Everybody believed that victory was already achieved, just waiting for the final bell.

Finally, the dictator has fled. Jubilation erupted. Hopes were high. Institutional reforms were expected. Thieves were likely to be punished.

Elections in 87, 92 and 95 for senate and house were widely welcomed. Young Turks who were perceived to be idealists and nationalists were the knights of our Camelot. 

The spice boys of Migz Zubiri, ace barbers, michael defensor among others and the bright boys of Chiz Escudero, Gibo Teodoro, Alan Cayetano among others were a refreshing sight. Bright, articulate and refined.

However, before the turn of the century. Rumors of a more sophisticated and more greedy young Turks began to surface.

It seems the hopes and aspirations of EDSA died long before it matured.

After 40 years, the manure the corrupt has produced is fertilizing the whole establishment. No wonder, we produced bright but crooked civil servants.

Hopefully, the idealism of the youth will carry out the reforms that we, their elders have failed to do.

Show us the way and we will follow.

We grew up in the same era. My family was one of the few who didn't go to EDSA, our grandparents were loyalist but my uncles and aunts weren't. To keep peace in the family, we barely spoke of politics during weekend gatherings. So when EDSA broke we stayed home. We cheered when Marcos left. But I often think the same thing, what did we gain after the revolution when corruption is rampant and a normal occurrence from government official to the most basic service Filipinos render. What did we do wrong? Where did we go wrong? Why do some "heroes" become the same people they used to fight? These are still questions I ask.

You either die a hero or you live long enough to see yourself become the villian - Harvey Dent

In our case, it seems the latter part of this quote is true to a lot of those who believed in EDSA.

Ang pagiging sakim ng tao sa pera at kapangyarihan ay siyang nagiging dahilan ng pagkasira ng sangkatauhan. Sana matuto na po tayo sa mga pagkakamali ng nakaraan upang hindi na natin ulit dadanasin sa kinabukasan.

I think the lesson in EDSA has its blessing and it's curse.... The blessing is.... We realizes that the will of the people has the power to topple even the most powerful person in the land. 

But the downside is that, it is up to the people if they want a change, if they are still voting for the same wrong people, that only means nothing will happen to change at all.

I was in EDSA in 1986 with my husband, pregnant with our first born - a son.

Morning Coffee Thoughts 2.0 this my first time posting a comment on your article. I love reading your pieces. I pay a lot of attention if its about the ICC case. Many times I wanted to comment and share my thoughts, then I hesitate because I might get comments I won’t like. This is me finally having the courage to post something I’d like to share. 

I hope someday all Filipinos realize that we have been manipulated too long, we were always divided. But its not left vs right, it has always been top vs bottom. 
And the division widened even more so when a powerful man normalized on tv horrible behaviors. This made us Filipinos spiral down morally because our mental perception about how justice is achieved has shifted. Because of his behavior and statements when he was in power, ordinary people were encourage to commit horrible behaviors. Like its acceptable to just point at someone and call them drug addicts and drug peddlers without any solid facts that its actually true, baseless rumors spreading from one mouth to another has been a credible source for anyone willing to spread it. We were manipulated to think its ok and normal, no credibility check necessary. And then, anyone related to so called salot sa lipunan are enablers. Anyone defending them are called pro addicts. Our emphaty as Filipinos towards our fellow Filipinos suffering went down the drain. We have blinded ourselves so much. I can understand the marites culture but when rumors start inciting violence, its no longer just a kwentong marites sa kanto. Someone has already ended up dead, and condemned even in death. 

I’d also like to encourage everyone here, if you haven’t watch it yet, please take the time to watch documentaries like the nat geo tv documentary the nightcrawlers. 
It tells the story of Filipino photo journalists documenting the crime scenes during the drug war.
May it restore some feelings of emphaty in our hearts and may we teach our younger generation that hurtful words, even delivered in a jokingly manner do hurt feelings of others. 

Many documentaries were made about the drug war, but sadly I think they were filtered and not allowed to be viewed in the Philippines.

What qualities do you believe the Phils most needs in 2028?

If we look at the current direction of the country, I believe the Philippines would most need these qualities in 2028:

1. Moral Integrity
A leader whose character is steady even when unseen. Corruption fatigue is real. The nation needs someone who values honesty over popularity and long-term good over short-term political gain.

2. Unifying Leadership
The country has become highly polarized in recent years. A president who can calm tensions, listen across political lines, and avoid governing through division would be crucial.

3. Economic Competence
Inflation, job security, food supply, and foreign investment remain pressing issues. The next president must understand economic fundamentals and surround themselves with capable technocrats.

4. Strong but Measured Governance
Firm leadership is appreciated by many Filipinos — but strength must be balanced with respect for democratic institutions, rule of law, and human rights.

5. Foreign Policy Balance
With tensions in the West Philippine Sea and global power shifts, the Philippines needs a leader who can:
Protect national sovereignty
Maintain strategic alliances
Avoid unnecessary escalation

6. Crisis Management Ability
The Philippines is disaster-prone. The next president must be calm, data-driven, and decisive during emergencies.

7. Heart for the Poor
Millions of Filipinos still struggle daily. Policies must go beyond rhetoric and genuinely uplift those in poverty — especially in education, healthcare, and livelihood.

In a nation often divided by political loyalties, it is worth pausing to recognize moments of quiet maturity. One such moment is Leni Robredo’s grace in accepting electoral defeat, her respect for the Office of the President, and her continued commitment to public service—now as Mayor of Naga City.

There is an old adage in public service that leadership is revealed not in victory, but in how one conducts oneself after loss. True statesmanship lies in choosing cooperation over resentment, and service over spectacle. This principle is precisely what Mayor Robredo demonstrates.

Extending courtesy to the sitting President is neither weakness nor surrender; it is a basic expression of political maturity. Respect for institutions must transcend personalities. Unfortunately, some choose to misinterpret civility as capitulation, or coordination as endorsement.

Yes, this is political—public office always is. But not every interaction must be reduced to factional conflict. Governance cannot function if every gesture is framed as one camp versus another. Courtesy and coordination between leaders are essential elements of responsible administration, not automatic declarations of political allegiance.

In local governance, it is standard practice to accord respect to the President—ally or opponent alike. This is not merely protocol; it is statesmanship. More importantly, it provides an opportunity to advocate for programs and resources that directly benefit local communities.

Mayor Robredo has consistently upheld this standard of professionalism. She previously received Sara Duterte with courtesy during an official visit to Naga. Now that Ferdinand Marcos Jr. is visiting the city, civility and cooperation should be expected—not questioned.

When national and local leaders work together despite political differences, the real beneficiaries are the people. The recent joint inspection highlights what effective governance should look like: practical coordination, evidence-based assessment, and timely action—particularly on urgent concerns such as flood mitigation and disaster preparedness.

What stands out is the focus on substance rather than spectacle. Flood control systems, evacuation facilities, and resilient housing are not partisan issues; they are matters of public safety and human dignity. If cooperation accelerates solutions and improves outcomes, then it is not only acceptable—it is commendable.

Ultimately, good governance is not measured by who receives credit, but by whether institutions function and lives are protected. More collaboration that is quiet, results-oriented, and people-centered can only strengthen public trust and democratic institutions.

At the end of the day, public service must always come before politics. 🇵🇭

Additional Academic Trivia : Philippines is the only SE Asian county with June to March school year.

In the Know: PH only country with June-March school year

The Philippines is the only member of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (Asean) that follows a June-to-March academic calendar. Other Asean countries, including Cambodia, Laos, Indonesia, Singapore, Malaysia, Vietnam and, most recently, Thailand, are already following the September-to-May school calendar.

In the United States, the school calendar begins between August and September and ends in June.

In some European countries, the school year starts in the first week of September. In Nordic countries like Denmark, Finland, Sweden, Norway and Iceland, it generally starts between mid- to end-August. In Southern European countries like Greece, Portugal and Turkey, the school calendar starts during the second half of September.

Other Asian countries like Japan and South Korea have a different school calendar. The academic year in Japan starts in April and ends in March. South Korea’s academic year begins in March and ends in February.

Like in some European countries and in the United States, China’s school calendar also starts in September and ends in June.

In Australia, the school year begins in January and ends in December.

Executive Order No. 292, signed in July 1987 by then President Corazon Aquino, provides that the opening date of the school year for the elementary, secondary and tertiary levels for public and private schools “shall not be earlier than the first day of June nor later than the last day of July of each year unless prevented by fortuitous events.”

This academic calendar was adopted to coincide with the country’s two seasons—rainy (from June to November) and dry (from December to May).

Education Secretary Armin Luistro said last month that no decision has been made regarding the proposed shift in academic calendar for basic education.

He cited a Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration (Pagasa) study showing that typhoons and heavy rains are not frequent occurrences during June and July.

Sources: Inquirer Archives, Organisation of School Time in Europe by the European Commission, Seoul National University, Chuo University, Australia.gov.au, NYC Department of Education
